Member-only story

Technology

Software Development

Software Engineering

Programming

Coding

Most developers don’t become better because they write more features. They improve because they make better decisions before they write the first line of code. The quality of a feature is often determined long before implementation begins.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

7 Decisions Senior Engineers Make Before Building a FeatureWhen I first started working as a software engineer, I believed the hardest part of software development was writing code. Whenever a new ticket appeared in my sprint, I immediately opened my editor and started thinking about classes, functions, APIs, and database queries. It felt productive because I was moving quickly, and seeing new code appear on my screen gave me the impression that progress was being made.
After spending several years maintaining production systems, I realized that many of my biggest mistakes had very little to do with programming syntax or framework knowledge. They happened because I made poor decisions before implementation even started. I misunderstood the actual problem, ignored edge cases, duplicated existing logic, or optimized for today’s requirement without considering how the feature would evolve. The code itself often worked exactly as intended. The thinking behind it was the real problem.
